title: Why Some Rocks Float on Water

lesson.learn:
Some rocks float because they are full of gas bubbles, making them much less dense than solid water. Pumice is the classic example. It forms when gas-rich lava cools quickly and traps holes inside the rock. Those tiny cavities lower the average density enough that the rock can float, at least for a while.

That makes pumice a great reminder that rocks are not all heavy and solid. Their internal structure matters just as much as their chemical makeup.
lesson.simpler: Pumice can float because gas bubbles inside the rock make it less dense than water.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Floating rocks are mostly about density, not magic.
lesson.deeperDive:
The key variable is vesicular texture, which means the rock contains many trapped bubbles. When magma decompresses quickly, dissolved gases come out of solution and expand. If the lava solidifies before the bubbles escape, the result is a light, porous rock. Eventually those cavities can fill with water, and the rock may sink or become waterlogged.

This is also why volcanic rocks can vary so much. Two samples may come from the same eruption but have very different textures depending on gas content, cooling speed, and pressure change.
lesson.funFact: Pumice is light enough to drift on water.

people: Fishermen and sailors - people who may see floating pumice rafts
Students - learners seeing density in action
places: Ocean surfaces - where pumice can drift
Lava flows - the source of vesicular rock
Rock museums - places where pumice is displayed
years: Ancient times - pumice used as an abrasive
Present day - pumice is a textbook example of low density
concepts: Vesicle - a gas bubble trapped in rock
Lava - molten rock at the surface
Porosity - the amount of empty space in rock
Floating - staying on top of water because of density
